summary:
The Galatian Invasion of Greece (279–277 BC) was a massive incursion by Celtic warrior bands from the Danube region into Macedonia and Greece, which devastated the northern Balkans, threatened Delphi, and was only halted by determined Greek resistance — before the invaders crossed into Asia Minor to become the Galatians of Anatolia.

The Gauls (Celts, called Galatians or Gauls in Greek sources) had been expanding from central Europe for over a century. The invasion of 279 BC, led by chieftains including Brennus, overran Macedonia and advanced into Greece. At Thermopylae they broke through Greek defenses (as the Persians had a century earlier in [[event:1007|Xerxes I Invasion of Greece]]). At Delphi, the invaders were repelled — according to Greek tradition with divine intervention; in reality probably through the fierce resistance of the [[event:1204|Aetolian League]] and natural phenomena (landslides and thunderstorms). The Galatian bands eventually crossed the Bosphorus into Asia Minor, where they settled in the Galatian plateau (modern central Turkey) and became the Galatians known from the New Testament. As mercenaries and raiders, they terrorized Hellenistic Asia Minor until the [[event:1043|Kingdom of Pergamon]] under Attalus I finally defeated them in the 240s BC, a victory commemorated in the famous Dying Gaul sculpture.
